label: DNS zone transfer
recommendation: CIS DNS server Benchmarks: allow TCP/53 only where zone transfers (AXFR/IXFR) are required; restrict transfers to known secondaries on trusted paths. NIST SP 800-53 SC-7: do not expose zone transfer services to untrusted or internet-facing segments.

label: TFTP
recommendation: Industry practice (CIS, vendor guides): TFTP is unauthenticated and cleartext—block UDP/69 from untrusted networks. Use SFTP, HTTPS, or signed software distribution instead.

label: Link (legacy)
recommendation: Retire legacy Link services per asset-inventory and CIS “unneeded services” guidance; if still required, isolate on dedicated management segments with explicit allow lists (NIST least-functionality principle).

label: SUN RPC portmapper
recommendation: CIS and DISA-style hardening: RPC portmapper must not be reachable from the internet; restrict to internal application tiers and deny at the perimeter (supports NIST SC-7 boundary enforcement).

label: BSD r-commands (rexec/rlogin/rshell)
recommendation: CIS Unix/Linux Benchmarks: disable r-commands; they lack modern authentication and encryption. Use SSH with key-based auth and vendor-supported remote-admin patterns (NIST IA-2, SC-8).

label: LPD (line printer)
recommendation: Limit LPD to internal print infrastructure only; align with CIS “network service minimization” and segment printers on trusted VLANs, not general-user or DMZ subnets.

label: UUCP
recommendation: Treat UUCP as legacy batch transfer; remove from perimeter per CIS unused-services guidance or restrict to explicitly authorized partners over private connectivity.

label: OpenWindows
recommendation: Block legacy OpenWindows; CIS and vendor sunset guidance favor encrypted, supported remote-display and management tools with current patching and MFA where applicable.

label: NFS
recommendation: CIS NFS guidance: keep NFS on private networks with export ACLs and, where available, Kerberos (sec=krb5*). Never expose NFS to the internet; enforce host-level and firewall segmentation (NIST SC-7, SC-28).

label: X11 display
recommendation: CIS workstation/server Benchmarks: disable X11 listening on untrusted interfaces; avoid forwarding X11 across hostile networks. Prefer VPN or SSH X11 forwarding only with explicit need and session hardening.

label: Small services (ports 1-20)
recommendation: NIST SP 800-41 and firewall best practice: default-deny inbound to privileged ports; explicitly allow only documented services. Tune this range to your CIS-aligned baseline and change-management records.

label: FTP
recommendation: CIS guidance: prefer SFTP or HTTPS for file transfer. If FTP is mandatory, use FTPS (explicit TLS), disable anonymous access, and restrict source IPs; log and monitor per NIST AU family controls.

label: SSH
recommendation: CIS SSH Benchmarks: enforce current ciphers/MACs, disable root login and empty passwords, use keys where policy allows, and limit exposure with bastion or jump hosts aligned to Zero Trust access patterns.

label: Telnet
recommendation: CIS and DISA STIGs: disable Telnet; credentials and sessions are cleartext. Replace with SSH and mutual authentication where required (NIST SC-8, SC-13 for cryptography in transit).

label: SMTP
recommendation: CIS mail-server guidance: dedicate inbound SMTP to hardened relays; disable open relay; enable TLS and authentication policies consistent with NIST SP 800-177 (email security) and vendor baselines.

label: Finger
recommendation: CIS: disable Finger; it publishes user/account metadata useful for reconnaissance. Aligns with NIST privacy and information-disclosure minimization for public-facing systems.

label: POP2/POP3
recommendation: Industry standard: publish POP3S/IMAPS at the edge with TLS 1.2+; restrict cleartext POP/IMAP to internal networks per CIS mail benchmarks and NIST SC-8.

label: NNTP
recommendation: If NNTP remains in use, contain on internal news servers only; uncommon on modern perimeters—align with CIS service-minimization and deny at internet edge.

label: NTP
recommendation: CIS NTP guidance: use internal stratum servers; disable or restrict mode 6/7; rate-limit and monitor UDP/123 for reflection/amplification abuse (documented in NTP BCP and security advisories).

label: NetBIOS (RPC)
recommendation: Microsoft/CIS Windows Benchmarks: block NetBIOS (TCP/135) from the internet; confine to domain/management segments with host firewalls and perimeter deny rules (NIST SC-7).

label: NetBIOS name/datagram
recommendation: Same as NetBIOS hardening: internal-only; never expose UDP 137–138 from untrusted WANs—consistent with CIS Windows Server Benchmarks and lateral-movement reduction guidance.

label: NetBIOS session
recommendation: CIS/Microsoft guidance: do not expose SMB/NetBIOS session ports directly; use VPN, Private Link, or supported SMB-over-QUIC paths instead of raw internet exposure.

label: IMAP
recommendation: Publish IMAPS (993) at the boundary; keep cleartext IMAP on internal networks only—per CIS Benchmarks and NIST SP 800-52 (TLS) for protocols in transit.

label: SNMP
recommendation: CIS SNMP guidance: prefer SNMPv3 (authPriv); disable SNMPv1/v2c on untrusted interfaces; restrict agents to NMS subnets. Maps to NIST SC-8 and monitoring segmentation.

label: BGP
recommendation: Industry BCP: BGP belongs only on routers and authorized peering links with TCP MD5 or TCP-AO and RPKI where deployed; never expose BGP sessions to general-purpose internet hosts (NIST SC-7).

label: LDAP
recommendation: CIS Active Directory / LDAP guidance: prefer LDAPS (636) or LDAP StartTLS at trust boundaries; bind cleartext LDAP to internal directory replicas and require TLS for queries crossing segments (NIST SC-8).

label: HTTPS
recommendation: Often legitimate public service; informational only. Still validate TLS configuration against CIS and NIST SP 800-52 (cipher suites, cert lifecycle, HSTS where appropriate).

label: SMB (Windows file sharing)
recommendation: Microsoft MSRC and CIS guidance: block SMB (TCP/445) from the internet; require VPN, Zero Trust file access, or SMB over QUIC rather than direct exposure (prevents ransomware-style entry).

label: Syslog
recommendation: NIST SP 800-92 and CIS logging guidance: send syslog over dedicated management networks; use TLS (e.g. RELP/TCP 6514) where possible; validate sender ACLs and integrity protections.

label: SOCKS proxy
recommendation: Authenticate SOCKS users, enforce source restrictions, and monitor for abuse—aligned with proxy hardening in CIS and NIST AC-3 (least privilege) for egress paths.

label: Alternate HTTP
recommendation: Non-standard HTTP ports require explicit change approval; document owners, apply WAF/reverse-proxy controls as for 443, and monitor—consistent with CIS web-server and NIST CM-2 baselines.

label: Alternate HTTP
recommendation: Non-standard HTTP ports require explicit change approval; document owners, apply WAF/reverse-proxy controls as for 443, and monitor—consistent with CIS web-server and NIST CM-2 baselines.

label: Alternate HTTP
recommendation: Non-standard HTTP ports require explicit change approval; document owners, apply WAF/reverse-proxy controls as for 443, and monitor—consistent with CIS web-server and NIST CM-2 baselines.

label: Cisco AUX-style
recommendation: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmarks: restrict device management to out-of-band networks, jump hosts, and explicit management VRFs; prohibit internet-facing auxiliary ports.

label: Cisco AUX stream-style
recommendation: Same as Cisco management-plane guidance: internal OOB or jump-host access only; align with CIS network-device Benchmarks and NIST SC-7 for management segmentation.

label: Cisco AUX binary-style
recommendation: Same as Cisco management-plane guidance: internal OOB or jump-host access only; align with CIS network-device Benchmarks and NIST SC-7 for management segmentation.

label: NFS lockd / nlockmgr
recommendation: Co-locate lockd controls with NFS: keep on internal networks with identical segmentation and export policies as port 2049 (CIS/NFS and NIST SC-7).

label: RDP
recommendation: Microsoft and CIS guidance: avoid internet-exposed RDP; use Remote Desktop Gateway, VPN, or Zero Trust brokers; enforce NLA, MFA, patching, and IP allow lists if exposure is unavoidable.

label: VNC
recommendation: CIS guidance: do not expose cleartext VNC; tunnel over SSH or VPN, or use products with built-in TLS and strong authentication (NIST SC-8).

label: Microsoft SQL Server
recommendation: Microsoft SQL Server CIS Benchmark: never publish SQL Server to the internet; use private connectivity, application tiers, and TLS; enforce least-privilege DB roles (NIST SC-7, AC-6).

label: MySQL / MariaDB
recommendation: CIS MySQL/MariaDB Benchmarks: bind to private interfaces, require TLS for remote connections, disable remote root, and allow only application subnets—no public listeners.

label: PostgreSQL
recommendation: CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark: listen_addresses restricted to localhost or private nets; use connection pooling and TLS; align with NIST SC-7 and database vendor hardening guides.

label: Redis
recommendation: Redis vendor security guidance and CIS-style exposure rules: never internet-facing; require AUTH, ACLs, and TLS; bind to loopback or private VLANs only.

label: MongoDB
recommendation: MongoDB CIS Benchmark: enable authentication, authorization, and encryption in transit; restrict to application subnets behind APIs and WAFs—no direct internet exposure.

label: Elasticsearch HTTP
recommendation: Elastic and CIS-style guidance: enable Elastic Security/HTTPS, strong auth, and network isolation; historical exposures drove industry recommendations to keep clusters off the public internet.

label: WinRM HTTP/HTTPS
recommendation: Microsoft WinRM hardening: limit to management VLANs; prefer HTTPS (5986) with trusted certs; use JEA and constrained endpoints per CIS Windows Benchmarks and NIST AC-6.
